clean_story:
Hiding by bending light. Mimicry without movement.

The term optical camouflage comes from "optics" (how light behaves) and "camouflage" (disguising appearance)—but instead of blending with colors, it tricks the eye by redirecting light itself.

A squid’s skin doesn’t just change color; it warps light. Tiny cells called iridophores act like adjustable mirrors, scattering photons to match their surroundings—or vanish entirely. Military prototypes use similar tricks: cameras project real-time background images onto a cloak, making wearers seem invisible. The catch? It only works from one angle, like a hologram that disappears when you step sideways.

Japan’s "invisibility cloak" in 2021 used retroreflective material—think a bike reflector, but covering a whole person—to bounce light back at its source. From the viewer’s perspective, the wearer blends into the scenery. Yet touch them, and the illusion breaks.

Three related subjects are adaptive animal skins, stealth drone coatings, and light as a weapon.
